Feed post

Suggested

Peppe Silletti

• 3rd+

Product Engineer for AI-native startups (open to part-time opportunities as IC or Coach) | Podcast Host @ “Product Engineers - Create Fiercely”

Visit my website

6d •

Two books changed how I think about building products: Teresa Torres' Continuous Discovery Habits and Dave Farley's Continuous Delivery. Most people read one or the other, but you should really read both to get the big picture.

Discovery tells you what to build. Delivery tells you how to ship it fast enough for your learning to compound. One without the other is half a loop, and half a loop is how companies keep building stuff nobody asked for.

I mapped how the core concepts connect, slide by slide. If you're building a product and feel like you're either moving fast but guessing, or researching forever but never shipping, you'll find this small guide helpful.

carousel-two-loops

11 pages

posts.html:
Feed post

Chandan Lal Patary

• 1st

Author of 11 Books on Leadership & Transformation | Helping Leaders Stay Relevant in the Age of AI | Enterprise Business Transformation Coach | Follow for Weekly Insights

6h •

Quibi raised $2 billion.

It had world-class leadership.
It had premium content.
It still failed—in just 6 months.

Why?
Not because of lack of funding.
Not because of lack of talent.

👉 Because it missed the fundamentals of product thinking.
Wrong customer understanding
Rigid technology decisions
No real feedback loops
Leadership disconnected from user reality
Weak product culture
Poor experience design

This is the hard truth most teams don’t talk about:
Products don’t fail at launch.
They fail in how they are built.

In my Product Owner Guidebook, I break this down into 8 critical pillars that define whether a product will scale—or collapse.
Because success is not about:
❌ features
❌ funding
❌ hype
It’s about alignment across:
✔ customer
✔ technology
✔ process
✔ leadership
✔ team
✔ culture
✔ marketing
✔ experience
What will you get from this guidebook?
👉 A clear way to diagnose why products fail
👉 A system to build scalable, customer-centric solutions
👉 A mindset shift from feature-building → product thinking
👉 Real-world insights you can apply immediately
Quibi is not just a failed startup story.
It’s a lesson every product leader must understand.
👉 Before your next product decision, ask yourself:
Are you building features… or building the right product?

#productowners #productmanagers

1 reaction1

posts.textPreview: Feed post Suggested Adrian Demian • 3rd+ AI Systems Arhitect | Writing about AI @ TheAIBuilder View my newsletter 4h • Follow One prompt. An AI team lead breaks it down. 5 agents work in parallel. This is already real. 🤖 🔴 You're not hiring more engineers. You're becoming a better manager of agents. But here's what viral tweets skip: the architecture has failure modes that will hit you at 3am. The 3 things that break: o Context Fragmentation -- Agent A changes a function signature. Agent B calls the old one. Zero awareness between them. 🔀 o Merge Hell -- overlapping write scopes is an architectural failure, not a bug. 💥 o Local vs. Global -- each agent optimizes locally. The system degrades globally. Goodhart's Law, agent edition. 📉 The real skill isn't prompting. It's work decomposit
posts.html:
Feed post

Suggested

Adrian Demian

• 3rd+

AI Systems Arhitect | Writing about AI @ TheAIBuilder

View my newsletter

4h •

One prompt. An AI team lead breaks it down. 5 agents work in parallel. This is already real. 🤖 🔴

You're not hiring more engineers. You're becoming a better manager of agents.

But here's what viral tweets skip: the architecture has failure modes that will hit you at 3am.

The 3 things that break:

o Context Fragmentation -- Agent A changes a function signature. Agent B calls the old one. Zero awareness between them. 🔀
o Merge Hell -- overlapping write scopes is an architectural failure, not a bug. 💥
o Local vs. Global -- each agent optimizes locally. The system degrades globally. Goodhart's Law, agent edition. 📉

The real skill isn't prompting. It's work decomposition. 🎯

Three rules we use at #AIGENTIVE:
o ONE agent = ONE module (no cross-cutting concerns)
o Define interfaces BEFORE spawning
o The team lead prompt IS your system design document

Cost math: 5 agents = ~4x API cost. Only worth it when you have 3+ genuinely independent workstreams. ⚖️

We're at 80% agent-delegated on new features. The remaining 20% -- interface definitions, architectural constraints -- is where engineering judgment still lives. 🏗️

What's ONE task in your sprint that could cleanly split across 3 agents? Drop it below -- I'll tell you if the scope holds. 👇

#AIAgents #MultiAgent #SoftwareEngineering #TheAIBuilder

posts.textPreview: Feed post Suggested Rutger Boels • 2nd Engineering Logic Systems Engineering at Scale 1d • Follow The most important tool in software engineering is not an AI model, a framework or a language. It is the mental model of the code you hold in your head. AI is changing software engineering. With it, a whole bunch of things we took for granted are suddenly shifting. One pattern I keep seeing is that - the quality of your mental model of your code state, - the quality of your mental model of your goals, - the quality of your mental model of coding best practices (including architecture!) in the age of AI - your ability to articulate that in terms of concrete actions and - how well you can check the output quality of coding agents to line up the code with your internal model model are the decidin
posts.html:
Feed post

Suggested

Rutger Boels

• 2nd

Engineering Logic Systems Engineering at Scale

1d •

The most important tool in software engineering is not an AI model, a framework or a language. It is the mental model of the code you hold in your head. AI is changing software engineering. With it, a whole bunch of things we took for granted are suddenly shifting. One pattern I keep seeing is that - the quality of your mental model of your code state, - the quality of your mental model of your goals,  - the quality of your mental model of coding best practices (including architecture!) in the age of AI - your ability to articulate that in terms of concrete actions and - how well you can check the output quality of coding agents to line up the code with your internal model model are the deciding factors for output quality as classically measured, as long as you always include governance-as-code to guide the agent and garantuee some syntactic checks. Anything else is, to my mind, secondary. The most important unlock are the mental models. People reporting good results with high quality code all report the same things: coding best practices matter a lot more, not less. The developer has to steer. Architecture is important. Note that most of them can articulate very clearly what they do, why they do it and what works and what does not. That is not an accident - its the sign of a clear understanding - a good mental model of coding in the age of AI. From personal experience I can attest that the quality of your own mental models is absolute key to writing good code. Coding agents are best viewed as a kind of amplification device for single developers, and a very powerful one. Put in incoherent things, out comes much more incoherence. Legacy code generation at machine speed. Put in coherent enough input as context and prompts, and out can come crystalline code. Noticeably NOT at machine speed, but still with sizeable speedups. Perhaps most wildly: the size of your speedup depends quite directly on the clarity of your mental models, the coherence of your best practices, the clarity of the code itself. We need to push best coding practices everywhere to get to enjoy the benefits of coding agents while controlling for the dangers of coding agents. Modularity, hexagonal architecture, maximal testing, good old clean code and clean architecture - all parts of this. And it all starts with clarity of thought much more than with the details of any particular coding agent or approach. Accessible today, if you put in the work. Please dont treat LLMs as magic random machines either for real *or* as an argument. As a mental model that is *both* wrong enough to be harmful. My current model in a nutshell: They are stochastic, not random. They can be reset by opening a new conversation. They simulate human intelligence. Their output depends on their inputs. They are context-indexed stochastic functors. Well-crafted code remains well-crafted code with very well-documented benefits. Those still hold. Gravity still exists :). What do you think?

16 reactions16

11 comments11 comments

posts.textPreview: Feed post Victoria Charra • 1st Founder @ Talent Wind ✨ Recruiting, People & Culture Visit my website 4h • Adding someone on LinkedIn is not networking. Networking is what happens after. Most people connect and disappear. The relationship sits dormant until they need something: a referral, a job, a favor... and suddenly there's a message in your inbox from someone you barely remember. Don't be that person. Landing in someone's inbox with intention means reaching out when you have nothing to ask. Sharing something relevant to them. Acknowledging a career move. Nurturing a network feels slow. It also happens to be the only kind that works when you actually need it. #networking #relationshipbuilding #careergrowth #linkedintips … more 10 reactions 10 2 comments 2 comments Like Comment Repost S
posts.html:
Feed post

Victoria Charra

• 1st

Founder @ Talent Wind ✨ Recruiting, People & Culture

Visit my website

4h •

Adding someone on LinkedIn is not networking.
Networking is what happens after.
Most people connect and disappear. The relationship sits dormant until they need something: a referral, a job, a favor... and suddenly there's a message in your inbox from someone you barely remember.
Don't be that person.
Landing in someone's inbox with intention means reaching out when you have nothing to ask. Sharing something relevant to them. Acknowledging a career move.
Nurturing a network feels slow. It also happens to be the only kind that works when you actually need it.

#networking #relationshipbuilding #careergrowth #linkedintips
